theoryUsed: Reina can spell most silent-letter words with scaffolded letter-by-letter support, and can recognize most high-frequency words fluently. The silent-letter group requires spelling-production evidence because the teacher heading explicitly names a pattern-based skill; the high-frequency group requires recognition and fluency evidence because the heading signals sight-word goals, not spelling production.
changeSummary: Active session plan set to assignment-plan-reina-99a544dd.

theoryUsed: Reina's homework presents two distinct learning purposes: silent-letter spelling production and high-frequency sight-word recognition. The teacher's labeling separates these groups intentionally. Silent-letter words (sign, know, write, etc.) require orthographic memory and spelling production because silent consonants are the pattern focus. High-frequency words (among, building, circle, etc.) are labeled for recognition fluency, not spelling production, unless evidence shows spelling is the gap. Because this is Reina's first session with these targets and no recent evidence exists, the board must establish baseline evidence for both groups before any route choice or mastery claims. The baseline will reveal which targets are weak, which are clean, and whether the two groups require different dosages or support levels.

theoryUsed: Reina's silent-letter spelling depends on explicit orthographic memory for kn-, wr-, -mb, and gn- patterns, while her high-frequency word reading depends on whole-word visual recognition. The two groups require different measurement instruments: spelling construction for silent letters (because the teacher heading and pattern focus signal production goals) and visible-read pronunciation for high-frequency words (because the teacher heading signals recognition/fluency goals, not spelling production).

theoryUsed: Reina can master the three silent letter spelling words (sign, know, write) and recognize the three high-frequency words (among, building, circle) by the May 29 test date with targeted baseline measurement, one reinforcement cycle, and transfer proof via generated quest.

Reina's homework separates silent letter spelling construction from high-frequency word recognition. The silent letter group requires independent spelling recall, while the high-frequency group requires automatic recognition and fluency. With 2 days until test and no prior evidence, today's session must establish a clean baseline for both groups, identify any weak targets, and prepare a transfer theory for Quest unlock.
